features.summary: UnitedHealth Group reported strong Q3 2021 results with revenues of $72B (11% growth) and raised full-year adjusted EPS guidance to $18.65-$18.90. OptumHealth led growth with 32% revenue increase and 37% earnings growth, while Medicare Advantage membership grew 745,000 year-to-date. The company expects over 900,000 total MA member additions and continues expanding value-based care arrangements serving 2.2 million people under fully accountable arrangements. Commercial membership grew over 330,000 with 7% revenue growth year-over-year.
features.management_outlook: Management provided early 2022 guidance suggesting current analyst consensus appears reasonable, positioned toward the upper end of their initial outlook range. They expect lower unfavorable COVID impacts than 2021 but acknowledge continued uncertainty. The Change Healthcare transaction is expected to close in first half of 2022. Long-term earnings growth outlook of 13-16% remains intact, supported by untapped collaborative potential between UnitedHealthcare and Optum, technology advancement opportunities, and consumer experience improvements.
features.risks: COVID-related impacts remain difficult to predict with dramatic variation over the past 20 months requiring prudent management approach. Labor market tightness across healthcare is creating staffing challenges and increased costs for network partners, which will influence rate negotiations. Regulatory uncertainty around the Change Healthcare transaction continues, and there are ongoing discussions around Medicare risk adjustment payments following recent media attention on the topic.
features.key_drivers: Value-based care expansion is a primary growth driver with OptumCare adding 8,000 of planned 10,000 new physicians year-to-date and strong retention rates. Digital-first product launches in 2022 offering integrated virtual and physical care with expected 15% price advantages. Home and community care platform expansion across all 50 states serving 7 million members, with patients showing 14% lower hospital admission rates and 4% higher physician encounter rates compared to traditional care models.
